title: The Instagram Home Performance
subtitle: A reading on what curated home content does to our relationship with our own homes
excerpt: A reading on the gap between the curated home online and the real home lived in. Examines how constant exposure to styled and selected images affects how we see our own spaces, and what it takes to stop performing home and start inhabiting it.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. Draws on cultural commentary about social media, domestic aesthetics, and the commercialisation of home.

practice.whenToUse: Use when scrolling home content has left you feeling dissatisfied with your own space, or when you find yourself planning your home around how it would look in a photo.

content.text: A reading on the curated home and what it costs. The online home is a genre with strict conventions: the neutral palette, the well-lit shelf, the vase with three stems, the absence of laundry and children's shoes. Constant exposure to this genre reshapes expectations of what a home should look like and creates a specific kind of dissatisfaction with the home you actually live in.
What the curated home actually is

The homes that appear well on social media have typically been tidied, styled, and photographed from the one angle that works. The rest of the room is often out of frame: the piled corner, the utility room, the ordinary chaos of a working household. What is presented as everyday life is usually a construction.

This is not a critique of people who post home content. The problem is structural: the platform selects for the beautiful. What you see is not a representative sample of how people actually live. It is the fraction of the fraction that photographs well, repeated thousands of times until it starts to feel like a norm.
What it does to your own home

When the comparison frame is a feed of styled images, the lived-in home starts to fail a test it was never designed for. A home that is warm, functional, and filled with life does not photograph the same way as one prepared for content. The evidence of living (the books on the floor, the child's drawing on the fridge, the chair that is comfortable but not beautiful) becomes something to apologise for or hide.

The result for many women is a persistent low-level discomfort with their own home: the sense that it is not quite right, not quite enough, not what it should be. The home becomes another site of the not-yet-arrived feeling that also attaches to the body, the career, the relationship.

The alternative is not to stop caring about how a home looks. It is to separate the question 'does this space work for the people who live in it?' from the question 'would this photograph well?'. The first question produces homes that are actually useful. The second produces homes that perform usefulness.

A home that is comfortable, personal, and imperfect in the ways that come from actual use is doing its job. The discomfort arrives from measuring it against a genre designed to sell products and generate engagement, not to describe how people live.
Written for homemade.education. Draws on cultural commentary about social media, domestic aesthetics, and the commercialisation of home.
